target: openclaw/openclaw github_issue #40332: [Feature]: Per-binding and per-agent permissionMode for ACP sessions
github_context:
GitHub item:
- Repository: openclaw/openclaw
- Type: issue
- Number: 40332
- URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/40332
- Title: [Feature]: Per-binding and per-agent permissionMode for ACP sessions
- State: OPEN
- Author: talison
- Labels: P2, clawsweeper:no-new-fix-pr, clawsweeper:fix-shape-clear, clawsweeper:needs-maintainer-review, clawsweeper:needs-product-decision, clawsweeper:needs-security-review, clawsweeper:source-repro, impact:security, issue-rating: 🦞 diamond lobster

Body:
```markdown
## Feature Request

### Problem
`permissionMode` for the acpx plugin is currently global — set at `plugins.entries.acpx.config.permissionMode` and applies to **all** ACP sessions on the gateway. There's no way to scope permissions per-agent or per-binding.

This becomes a problem when running multiple ACP agents with different trust levels. For example:
- A DevOps agent that needs `approve-all` (full file write + shell access)
- A research agent that should only need `approve-reads`
- A code review agent that needs reads + limited writes but no shell exec

Currently, if any one agent needs `approve-all`, every agent gets it.

### Proposed Solution
Allow `permissionMode` as an override at two levels, following the existing ACP override precedence:

1. **Per-binding**: `bindings[].acp.permissionMode`
2. **Per-agent runtime**: `agents.list[].runtime.acp.permissionMode`
3. **Global fallback** (current): `plugins.entries.acpx.config.permissionMode`

This matches the existing precedence chain for `mode`, `cwd`, `backend`, and `label`.

### Example Config
```jsonc
{
  "bindings": [
    {
      "type": "acp",
      "agentId": "devops",
      "match": { "channel": "telegram", "peer": { "id": "-100xxx:topic:42" } },
      "acp": {
        "agent": "claude",
        "permissionMode": "approve-all"  // full access for devops
      }
    },
    {
      "type": "acp",
      "agentId": "reviewer",
      "match": { "channel": "discord", "peer": { "id": "333333" } },
      "acp": {
        "agent": "claude",
        "permissionMode": "approve-reads"  // read-only for reviewer
      }
    }
  ],
  "plugins": {
    "entries": {
      "acpx": {
        "config": {
          "permissionMode": "approve-reads"  // safe default
        }
      }
    }
  }
}
```

### Use Case
Personal multi-agent setup with a Telegram forum group. Each topic is bound to a different ACP agent (DevOps, Strategy, etc.). The DevOps agent needs full shell/write access, but other agents should be restricted. Without per-binding scoping, the only option is `approve-all` globally, which over-provisions every agent.

### Additional Context
- Related: #39535 (permissionMode config validation issues)
- The `nonInteractivePermissions` setting (`fail` vs `deny`) would also benefit from per-binding scoping for the same reasons.
```

browser status now emits layered control-ui/profile/CDP diagnostics and cleaner HTML upstream errors; cron force-runs now use a detached manual lane so nested cron work can still reuse the cron execution lane. - What did NOT change (scope boundary): this does not add new browser capabilities, does not change sandbox policy beyond PATH recovery for local fallback, and does not redesign cron scheduling semantics. ## Change Type (select all) - [x] Bug fix - [ ] Feature - [ ] Refactor - [ ] Docs - [ ] Security hardening - [ ] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [x] Gateway / orchestration - [x] Skills / tool execution - [x] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [ ] Integrations - [x] API / contracts - [x] UI / DX - [ ] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Closes #41549 - Closes #41553 - Closes #41558 ## User-visible / Behavior Changes - Exec tool can now find login-shell-managed binaries when the default sandbox host falls back to direct local execution. - `openclaw browser status` now surfaces layered diagnostics for Control UI auth/origin configuration, browser profile attach mode, and remote/local CDP reachability. - Browser HTTP errors now summarize proxy HTML error pages instead of dumping raw markup. - Detached `cron.run --force` no longer self-deadlocks when isolated cron work reuses the cron execution lane. ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? This PR addresses three targeted bug fixes: (1) login-shell PATH recovery now triggers when `host === "sandbox"` falls back to direct local execution (not just `host === "gateway"`), (2) `openclaw browser status` gains layered diagnostics covering Control UI auth/origin config, profile attach-mode, and CDP reachability, with HTML proxy error pages now summarized instead of dumped raw, and (3) `cron.run --force` is moved to a new `CommandLane.CronManual` lane so isolated cron jobs can still enqueue on `CommandLane.Cron` without self-deadlocking. **Key changes:** - `bash-tools.exec.ts`: extends `host === "gateway"` PATH probe guard to also cover `host === "sandbox"` when no sandbox runtime exists - `status-diagnostics.ts` (new): pure-logic module for `deriveBrowserStatusDiagnostics`, `deriveGatewayControlUiDiagnostics`, and `combineBrowserStatusDiagnostics`; It does not exercise the full control UI bundle, so paying the full `pnpm ui:build` cost there is unnecessary. Release and normal Docker builds are unchanged because the new path is opt-in and only enabled by the smoke workflow. ## Testing - `./node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check Dockerfile .github/workflows/install-smoke.yml` ``` Comments/context: ```markdown - greptile-apps at 2026-03-10T12:22:48Z:

Greptile Summary

This PR aims to speed up the `Install Smoke` CI workflow by skipping the expensive `pnpm ui:build` step when building smoke Docker images, replacing it with a minimal placeholder `index.html`. The approach is sound — the workflow correctly passes `OPENCLAW_SKIP_UI_BUILD=1` as a build-arg, and the conditional shell logic in the `Dockerfile` is well-structured. **However, there is a critical bug that makes the optimization a no-op:** - `OPENCLAW_SKIP_UI_BUILD` is declared on line 17 as a **global** `ARG` (before the first `FROM`). In Docker multi-stage builds, global ARGs are scoped exclusively to `FROM` instructions — they are **not** inherited by any subsequent build stage. - Because the `build` stage never re-declares `ARG OPENCLAW_SKIP_UI_BUILD`, the variable is always empty inside that stage's `RUN` commands, so `[ -n "$OPENCLAW_SKIP_UI_BUILD" ]` is always false, `pnpm ui:build` always runs, and the intended speedup is never achieved. - The fix is one line: add `ARG OPENCLAW_SKIP_UI_BUILD` inside the `build` stage immediately before the conditional `RUN` (see inline comment for a suggested fix). This is the same pattern already used by `OPENCLAW_EXTENSIONS`, which is declared globally on line 15 and re-declared inside `ext-deps` on line 29.

Confidence Score: 1/5

ENV OPENCLAW_PREFER_PNPM=1 -RUN pnpm ui:build +RUN if [ -n "$OPENCLAW_SKIP_UI_BUILD" ]; then \ + mkdir -p dist/control-ui && \ + printf '%s\n' 'OpenClaw Control UI

' > dist/control-ui/index.html; \ + else \ + pnpm ui:build; \ + fi # Prune dev dependencies and strip build-only metadata before copying # runtime assets into the final image. target: openclaw/openclaw github_issue #42408: [Bug/Docs]: local+hybrid memory_search quality can appear unstable due to extraPaths path drift + benchmark-file contamination
github_context:
GitHub item:
- Repository: openclaw/openclaw
- Type: issue
- Number: 42408
- URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/42408
- Title: [Bug/Docs]: local+hybrid memory_search quality can appear unstable due to extraPaths path drift + benchmark-file contamination
- State: OPEN
- Author: 1sexywoo8
- Labels: P2, clawsweeper:no-new-fix-pr, clawsweeper:needs-maintainer-review, clawsweeper:needs-product-decision, clawsweeper:needs-live-repro, impact:session-state, issue-rating: 🐚 platinum hermit

Body:
```markdown
### Summary
On OpenClaw `2026.3.8` (macOS arm64), we observed that `memory_search` quality under `provider=local` + `hybrid` can look severely degraded (including empty/irrelevant top hits), but root cause was a **combination** of:

1. `extraPaths` path drift (configured paths not aligned with the active main workspace)
2. benchmark/evaluation files being indexed and contaminating retrieval ranking
3. strict scoring sensitivity to path/term matching

After fixing path alignment and removing benchmark contamination from retrieval context, quality recovered substantially.

### Environment
- OpenClaw: `2026.3.8`
- OS: macOS arm64
- Memory provider: `local`
- Local model: `hf:ggml-org/embeddinggemma-300m-qat-q8_0-GGUF/embeddinggemma-300m-qat-Q8_0.gguf`
- Store: sqlite
- Query mode: hybrid

### Initial Symptoms
- `openclaw memory search --json` often returned irrelevant top hits
- In one tuning round, some queries appeared empty or near-empty relevance
- Strict benchmark score dropped despite successful indexing

### What We Found
1. `openclaw memory status --deep --json` revealed path issues (`additional memory path missing`) when `extraPaths` were not valid for the active workspace.
2. A benchmark/queryset markdown file was indexed and surfaced as top result for multiple queries, inflating/contaminating evaluations.
3. Broad `extraPaths` increased noise in retrieval candidates.

### Repro (conceptual)
1. Configure `provider=local`, hybrid enabled.
2. Add `extraPaths` that are valid in legacy workspace but not in current active workspace.
3. Include benchmark/queryset docs in indexed paths.
4. Reindex (`openclaw memory index --force`) and run semantic queries.
5. Observe unstable/irrelevant ranking.

### Mitigations that worked
- Use **absolute** existing paths in `extraPaths` (verify with deep status).
- Narrow `extraPaths` to high-signal docs only.
- Exclude benchmark/queryset artifacts from retrieval corpus.
- Reindex after path changes.

### Expected Behavior
- Better diagnostics/warnings when `extraPaths` are likely misaligned with active workspace
- Optional ability to exclude files/patterns from memory indexing (e.g., benchmark docs)
- Guidance in docs for avoiding benchmark contamination and path drift in multi-workspace setups

### Actual Behavior
- Retrieval quality appears model/provider-bad when root cause is often data/path hygiene.

### Request
Could we add:
1. `memory index/search` docs section for path hygiene + evaluation contamination pitfall

[body truncated after 2500 chars]
```

Greptile Summary

This PR fixes the root cause of #42072 by introducing `loadInternalHooksForStartup`, a startup-specific loader that registers workspace-local hooks for every configured agent workspace (not just the default) and wraps each workspace handler with a scope guard so hooks only fire for events belonging to that workspace. Shared hooks (managed, bundled, extra dirs) are loaded once and suppressed per-workspace when a local override exists. **Key findings:** - The shared and per-workspace hook registrations are wrapped in a single `try-catch` block. Greptile Summary

This PR hardens the context-overflow recovery loop in the embedded PI runner by adding one suppress-hook retry (suppressing external memory/prompt-hook injections) as a last-resort fallback before giving up, and normalises `model_context_window_exceeded` / `context_window_exceeded` stop-reason strings into the existing overflow handling path across error formatting, failover classification, and user-facing text sanitisation. - **Suppress-hook retry** (`run.ts`, `attempt.ts`, `types.ts`): adds a single bounded retry with `suppressPromptHookContext: true` after compaction and tool-truncation recovery both fail. Correctly guarded by `!isCompactionFailure` to preserve `compaction_failure` semantics. - **Stop-reason normalisation** (`errors.ts`, `failover-matches.ts`): extends `CONTEXT_OVERFLOW_ERROR_HEAD_RE` and the failover `timeout` pattern to recognise raw stop-reason strings; `formatRawAssistantErrorForUi` now returns friendly copy for these variants early. - **Flag persistence concern**: `promptHookContextSuppressedForOverflow` is set to `true` and never reset. Within a single `runEmbeddedPiAgent` call, all subsequent `runEmbeddedAttempt` invocations (i.e. every follow-up prompt after tool calls) will also run with hooks suppressed — even if the suppress retry succeeded and the overflow is resolved. For long tool-call chains this silently disables memory/hook context for all remaining turns. target: openclaw/openclaw github_pr #45200: fix(subagents): notify user on announce give-up instead of silently dropping result
github_context:
GitHub item: - Repository: openclaw/openclaw - Type: pull_request - Number: 45200 - URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/45200 - Title: fix(subagents): notify user on announce give-up instead of silently dropping result - State: OPEN - Author: waliddafif - Labels: agents, size: S, triage: needs-real-behavior-proof, mantis: telegram-visible-proof, P1, rating: 🧂 unranked krab, merge-risk: 🚨 session-state, merge-risk: 🚨 message-delivery, status: 📣 needs proof - Changed file count available to wrapper: 1 - Changed files: src/agents/subagent-registry.ts Body: ```markdown ## Problem When the subagent announce retry budget (`MAX_ANNOUNCE_RETRY_COUNT = 3`) is exhausted, `logAnnounceGiveUp` logs a warning, marks the run done, and the completed result is **permanently discarded with no indication to the user**. This is Pattern 1 from #44925 — documented across 9+ production incidents (E31, E42, E45, E79, E42 × n...): ``` Subagent completion direct announce failed Subagent announce give up (retry-limit) Subagent orphan run pruned ... reason=missing-session-entry ``` The user has no idea the task completed. They wait indefinitely or manually ask "what happened?". ## Fix Add `notifyAnnounceGiveUp(entry)`, called fire-and-forget from the `retry-limit` branch in `resumeSubagentRun()`. When `entry.requesterOrigin` carries a deliverable `channel` + `to` address (captured at spawn time, already stored in `SubagentRunRecord`), it calls the gateway `agent` method with `deliver: true` to push the result **directly to the user's channel**, bypassing the session-based announce pipeline that just failed. The notification surfaces: - a clear warning that delivery retried and failed - the task label - the recovered result text from `entry.frozenResultText` (when available) ## Behaviour | Scenario | Before | After | |---|---|---| | announce retry-limit hit, origin has channel+to | silent drop | best-effort last notify to user | | announce retry-limit hit, no usable origin | silent drop | silent drop (unchanged) | | last-resort notify also fails | silent drop | silent drop + audit log entry | Failure is intentionally swallowed — if the last-resort delivery also fails, the outcome is identical to the previous behaviour but the attempt is auditable. ## Scope This targets Pattern 1 (silent drop after retry-limit). Pattern 2 (timeout with no auto-restart) and Pattern 3 (file creation claims unverified) are separate issues. Related: #44925, #44919, #43367 ``` Comments/context: ```markdown - greptile-apps at 2026-03-13T14:55:11Z:

Greptile Summary

This PR addresses a real production pain point: when a subagent's announce retry budget is exhausted, the completed result was silently discarded with no user feedback. The fix adds a best-effort last-resort notifier (`notifyAnnounceGiveUp`) that, when the requester's delivery context is available, fires a one-shot gateway call to push a failure notice directly to the user's channel. Key observations: - **Missing `idempotencyKey`** — The `callGateway` params in `notifyAnnounceGiveUp` omit `idempotencyKey`, which the gateway `agent` handler assigns to both its deduplication key and `runId`. Without it, all concurrent give-up notifications share the same dedupe entry (`"agent:undefined"`), meaning a second concurrent call may be silently dropped, and `dispatchAgentRunFromGateway` receives `undefined` as `runId`, risking downstream failures. - **Indirect delivery via LLM session** — The implementation routes `notifyText` as a user message through the requester agent's session rather than delivering it verbatim. The user receives the LLM's response to the notification. In the dominant failure scenario (`missing-session-entry`), the session is reconstructed from scratch with no history, so the LLM processes the notification out of context. - No new tests cover `notifyAnnounceGiveUp` or the `retry-limit` branch that invokes it.

This PR fixes both the symptom and one of the root causes. ## Changes ### Layer 1 — Friendly error message (`errors.ts`) Added a dedicated check in `formatAssistantErrorText()` **before** the generic `invalidRequest` handler. When the raw error matches the `tool_use`/`tool_result` mismatch pattern, the user now sees: ``` Session history has a tool call mismatch — please try again. If this persists, use /new to start a fresh session. ``` Instead of the raw Anthropic API message: ``` LLM request rejected: messages.27: `tool_use` ids were found without `tool_result` blocks immediately after: exec17734030655683... ``` ### Layer 3 — Last-message edge case fix (`turns.ts`) `stripDanglingAnthropicToolUses()` previously short-circuited with "keep as-is" whenever the next message was not a user message. This meant that when the assistant message with dangling `tool_use` blocks was the **last message** in the array (no following message at all), the orphaned blocks were never stripped — causing the Anthropic API to reject the conversation on the very next turn. The fix distinguishes between two cases: - **Next message exists but is not `user`** → keep as-is (cannot repair pairing) - **No next message** → strip dangling `tool_use` blocks (no user turn could ever supply `tool_result`) ## Testing ```bash pnpm vitest run src/agents/pi-embedded-helpers.formatassistanterrortext pnpm vitest run src/agents/pi-embedded-helpers.validate-turns ``` 4 new tests added: - Anthropic verbatim error payload → friendly message - `tool_result` without `tool_use` variant → friendly message - Last assistant message with mixed content → `tool_use` stripped, text preserved - Last assistant message with only `tool_use` → replaced with `[tool calls omitted]` fallback All 48 tests pass. ``` Comments/context: ```markdown - greptile-apps at 2026-03-13T19:07:25Z:

- State: OPEN
- Author: mcfex

Body:
```markdown
### Summary

The webchat "Read aloud" button and "Voice input" mic both use the browser's built-in Web Speech APIs (SpeechSynthesis and Web Speech API). This completely ignores any TTS/STT configuration in openclaw.json, making self-hosted voice setups invisible to the webchat frontend.

### Problem to solve

Users who have configured messages.tts with a self-hosted OpenAI-compatible endpoint (e.g. Speaches/Kokoro) get no benefit from that setup in webchat. The "Read aloud" button calls window.speechSynthesis directly, bypassing the gateway entirely and using whatever OS voice is active — often the wrong language and voice. Similarly, the mic button sends audio to Google's Web Speech API regardless of whether a local Whisper-compatible STT endpoint is available.

### Proposed solution

- When messages.tts is configured, the "Read aloud" button should POST to the gateway's TTS endpoint and play back the returned audio blob, instead of using window.speechSynthesis

- Introduce a messages.stt config block (mirroring the existing messages.tts.openai structure) that, when set, routes webchat mic input through the configured STT endpoint (/v1/audio/transcriptions) instead of the browser's Web Speech API

### Alternatives considered

Keeping a separate external voice UI that intercepts messages and handles TTS/STT independently. This works but is fragile, requires maintaining a parallel stack, and duplicates functionality that OpenClaw should own natively.

### Impact

Self-hosted and privacy-conscious users running local TTS/STT (Speaches, Whisper, Kokoro, etc.) currently get zero benefit from their setup in webchat. This change would make webchat a first-class voice interface for anyone running an OpenAI-compatible audio stack — no cloud, no browser STT, full control.

### Evidence/examples

_No response_

### Additional information

_No response_
```

- `ui/src/ui/chat/speech.ts` implements STT with `SpeechRecognition` / `webkitSpeechRecognition` and TTS with `speechSynthesis`
- `ui/src/ui/chat/grouped-render.ts` wires the assistant “Read aloud” button to `speakText(...)`
- `ui/src/ui/views/chat.ts` wires the mic button to `startStt(...)`

So the core report is valid.

That said, I would be careful about the proposed config/API shape:

1. TTS likely does **not** need a new HTTP endpoint or new config
   Gateway already exposes TTS RPC in `src/gateway/server-methods/tts.ts`, including `tts.convert`, and existing TTS config already lives under `messages.tts`.
   So the smallest TTS fix looks like:
   - when WebChat/Control UI wants playback, call existing `tts.convert`
   - fetch/play the returned audio file/blob
   - only fall back to browser `speechSynthesis` when gateway TTS is unavailable or unsupported

2. STT probably should **not** introduce a parallel `messages.stt` block
   The repo already has a central audio transcription config surface under `tools.media.audio`, and the runtime entry point is `src/media-understanding/transcribe-audio.ts`.
   Introducing `messages.stt` would create a second audio-provider config path next to the one th

Body:
```markdown
### Summary

Sandboxed OpenClaw sessions should be able to spawn ACP sessions.
It's currently a [documented limitation](https://docs.openclaw.ai/tools/acp-agents#sandbox-compatibility) that breaks the use of 2 very important openclaw features - sandboxing and ACP.

ACP is great for better interop with coding harnesses, while sandboxing reduces blast radius when they make mistakes!

Continuation of #32935

### Problem to solve

Users running OpenClaw inside Docker containers (sandboxed) cannot use ACP at all. Sandboxed sessions cannot spawn ACP sessions because runtime='acp' runs on the host. This forces a binary choice: give up sandboxing (weakening security) or give up ACP orchestration (losing the ability to control Claude Code, Codex, etc. from within OpenClaw). For users who run OpenClaw in Docker for security isolation but want to orchestrate coding agents on the host, there's no middle ground.


### Proposed solution

Options:
1. Allow running ACP boxes in their own sandboxes, similar to sub-agents - re-use the same docker-based spawning method.
2. Allow running ACP INSIDE the session/bot sandboxes - they're already sandboxed, but ACP provides the neat interop layer.
3. Introduce a controlled bridge/proxy mechanism that lets sandboxed sessions request ACP spawns through a narrow, auditable API surface  - ACP proxy daemon on the host that accepts spawn/steer/cancel requests from the sandboxed container over a Unix socket or localhost port, with allowlisting by agent ID and rate limiting.  - sandbox: "bridge" mode for sessions_spawn that explicitly opts into host-side ACP execution with logged privilege escalation, so operators can audit what crossed the boundary.  - Config flag like acp.allowFromSandbox: true (with appropriate warnings) that relaxes the current blanket block for users who accept the tradeoff. A proxy/bridge approach would preserve the security boundary while enabling the orchestration use case. The key constraint: the bridge should be opt-in, auditable, and narrowly scoped (only ACP spawn/steer/cancel, not general host access).- borrowed from: #32935


### Alternatives considered

• Running OpenClaw unsandboxed: works but removes the security benefits of containerization entirely.
• Ditching ACP - I keep the security of sandboxes, and run coding harnesses inside existing sandbox, but no ACP interop.
• Using runtime: "subagent" instead: works for OpenClaw-native tasks but doesn't support external coding harnesses (Claude Code, Codex, G

[body truncated after 2500 chars]
```

- Thinkscape at 2026-03-14T07:29:01Z:
@willmhawkins you've mentioned https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/32935#issuecomment-3990375044 but you haven't explained how you've worked around the sandboxed agent interop. Do you just tell your agents to `exec+elevate` to run the ai coding cli on the gateway?

- dsantoreis at 2026-03-14T07:37:16Z:
This is a real architectural tension. The sandbox boundary is process-level isolation (Docker), but ACP sessions need host access to spawn coding harnesses (Claude Code, Codex, etc.).

Potential approaches:

1. **Socket forwarding**: expose a Unix socket from the host ACP runtime into the sandbox container via volume mount. The sandboxed session sends spawn requests over the socket, the host-side daemon executes them. This preserves sandbox isolation for the agent's own operations while allowing controlled ACP orchestration.

2. **Sidecar pattern**: run the ACP harness manager as a sidecar container with host-level access, communicating with the sandboxed OpenClaw container over a private network. The sandboxed session gets ACP capability without breaking its own isolation.

3. **Nested sandbox**: spawn ACP sessions in their own isolated containers (rather than on the host), with a shared workspace volume. This keeps everything containerized but requires Docker-in-Docker or a container runtime accessible from within the sandbox.

Option 1 is probably the most pragmatic path — minimal surface area, clear trust boundary, and doesn't r

github_context:

Per-request `dispatcher` overrides the global `EnvHttpProxyAgent`, so there is no double-proxy routing. - `rest-fetch.ts`: `ProxyAgent` constructor updated to pass `{ uri, connect: { keepAlive: false } }`. - Test files: mocks updated to accept the new constructor signature; `resolveEnvHttp [comments/context truncated after 1500 chars] ``` Diff/context: ```diff Subject: [PATCH 1/2] fix(gateway/discord): respect env proxy vars and prevent Gateway startup now bootstraps the global undici EnvHttpProxyAgent so Node's fetch() honors https_proxy/HTTP_PROXY. Bootstraps global EnvHttpProxyAgent in gateway startup so Node fetch() honors https_proxy/HTTP_PROXY env vars, and disables keepAlive on proxy agents to prevent ECONNRESET from local proxies (Clash, Surge, etc.) that close HTTP CONNECT tunnels after one request.

per_label_rationale.config: It makes gateway/Discord networking honor env proxy vars and fall back when explicit channels.discord.proxy is absent.
per_label_rationale.gateway: Gateway startup bootstraps the global undici proxy dispatcher and Discord gateway plugin behavior is changed.
per_label_rationale.reliability: The PR addresses timeouts and ECONNRESET from local proxies by disabling keepAlive.

excluded_label_rationale.security: Although proxy routing can affect security boundaries, the central change is connectivity/reliability and env configuration, not auth, secrets, or vulnerability hardening.

github_context:
GitHub item:
- Repository: openclaw/openclaw
- Type: issue
- Number: 48580
- URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/48580
- Title: Bug: acpx codex sessions 创建的会话立即退出 - stdin is not a terminal
- State: OPEN
- Author: lonelybeanz
- Labels: P2, clawsweeper:no-new-fix-pr, clawsweeper:needs-maintainer-review, clawsweeper:needs-live-repro, impact:session-state, issue-rating: 🐚 platinum hermit

Body:
```markdown
## Bug 描述

`acpx codex sessions new` 创建的 Codex 会话立即退出，进程 PID 存在但实际未运行。

## 复现步骤

1. 运行 `acpx codex sessions new --name test`
2. 检查会话状态：`.closed = false`，但进程已退出

## 错误信息



Codex CLI 需要 TTY，但 acpx 启动时未正确分配终端。

## 环境信息

- OpenClaw: v2026.3.13
- acpx: 0.1.15
- codex-cli: 0.114.0
- macOS: Darwin 24.6.0

## 日志

```
[acpx] spawning agent: npx @zed-industries/codex-acp
[acpx] agent advertised auth methods [chatgpt, codex-api-key, openai-api-key] but no matching credentials found — skipping (agent may handle auth internally)
[acpx] initialized protocol version 1
[acpx] created session test (019cf75b-34f4-73b3-8d3e-a87ca0f33bff)
[acpx] agent: codex
[acpx] cwd: /Users/moltbot/.openclaw/workspace-dev
```

会话 JSON 显示 `closed: false`，`pid: 51439`，但进程实际不存在。

## 期望行为

acpx 应正确处理 Codex CLI 对 TTY 的需求，或在检测到进程退出时自动关闭会话。
```

Comments/context:
```markdown
- Ryce at 2026-03-17T00:35:08Z:
This is session-state-drift at its most literal: the session JSON says `closed: false` and `pid: 51439`, but the process doesn't exist. Two sources of truth (session metadata vs OS process table) disagree, and nothing reconciles them.

The TTY requirement is the proximate cause, but the deeper issue is that acpx trusts the spawn result without verifying the process is actually alive. If the agent advertises auth methods but has no credentials, it should either fail hard or mark the session as failed — not create a zombie session entry that looks healthy in status output.

This pattern keeps appearing across the codebase: config says one thing, runtime does another, and the user discovers the drift only when something breaks. A session health check that diffs session state against actual process status would catch this at creation time instead of at failure time.

For anyone running ACPX Codex in production: if your sessions silently die without cleanup, you need a backup of your session state to know what was supposed to be running. keepmyclaw.com — encrypted backup for OpenClaw agents.


- clawsweeper at 2026-04-28T21:11:13Z:
Codex review: keeping this open for maintainer follow-up; there is still a little grit to resolve.

**Workflow note:** Future ClawSweeper reviews update this same comment in place.

<details>
<summary>How this review workflow works</summary>

Session health check is a secondary concern beyond the TTY root cause.

This works for simple commands but is slow and wasteful for pure-navigation menus (multi-level menu trees with back buttons, sub-menus, etc.) that don't need AI processing. OpenClaw's built-in `/model` command already implements this pattern (multi-level inline keyboard with callback routing) but the capability isn't exposed to user-defined commands. ## Changes **4 files, +72 lines:** - `src/config/zod-schema.providers-core.ts` — Schema: adds optional `menus` and `routes` to `TelegramCustomCommandSchema` - `src/config/types.telegram.ts` — Type: updates `TelegramCustomCommand` with menu/route fields - `extensions/telegram/src/bot-native-commands.ts` — Registers `bot.command()` handler for customCommands with menus, sending `menus.main` as inline keyboard - `extensions/telegram/src/bot-handlers.ts` — In `callback_query` handler, routes callback data matching `routes` config to menu navigation (editMessage), unmatched callbacks fall through to AI ## Config Example ```json { "customCommands": [{ "command": "work", "description": "Work menu", "menus": { "main": { "text": "⚡ *Work Menu*", "buttons": [ [{"text": "📊 Status", "callback_data": "w:status"}], [{"text": "🔧 Tools", "callback_data": "w:tools"}] ] }, "tools": { "text": "⚡ *Work Menu* › *Tools*", "buttons": [ [{"text": "◀️ Back", "callback_data": "w:nav:main"}] ] } }, "routes": { "w:tools": "tools", "w:nav:main": "main" } }] } ``` - `menus`: defines menu pages (`main` is the entry point) - `routes`: maps callback_data → menu name for navigation (handled at gateway level, no AI) - Callback data **not** in routes falls through to AI for action execution ## Behavior 1. User sends `/work` → bot responds instantly with `menus.main` inline keyboard 2. User clicks "Tools" (`w:tools`) → callback matches `routes`, message is edited to show `tools` menu 3. User clicks "Status" (`w:status`) → not in routes, forwarded to AI agent as a synthetic message 4. User clicks "Back" (`w:nav:main`) → callback matches routes, message edited back to ma [body truncated after 2500 chars] ``` Comments/context: ```markdown - greptile-apps at 2026-03-17T09:06:25Z:

Greptile Summary

This PR extends `customCommands` in the Telegram provider with optional `menus` and `routes` fields, enabling declarative multi-level inline keyboard menus that are handled at the gateway level without an AI roundtrip. The approach follows the pattern already used by the built-in `/model` command and is a clean, additive change with no impact on existing commands. Key findings from the review: - **Weak button schema validation** (`zod-schema.providers-core.ts`): The `buttons` field uses `z.array(z.array(z.any()))`, skipping per-button validation. Malformed button configs (missing `callback_data`, wrong types) pass Zod and only fail at runtime when Telegram rejects the API call. A structured `z.object({ text: z.string(), callback_data: z.string() })` schema would catch misconfiguration early. - **Silent callback data collision** (`bot-handlers.ts`): The routing loop breaks on the first command whose `routes` map contains the matching key. If two `customCommands` share the same callback_data string (e.g. both use `"back": "main"`), the second command's menu is silently never reached. There is no validation at config load time to detect this. The namespaced key convention in the PR description is the right instinct but isn't enforced. - **`(c: any)` casts** (`bot-handlers.ts`, `bot-native-commands.ts`): `TelegramCustomCommand` already carries the new optional fields after this PR, so the `any` casts are unne [comments/context truncated after 1500 chars] ``` Diff/context: ```diff --- diff --git a/extensions/telegram/src/bot-handlers.runtime.ts b/extensions/telegram/src/bot-handlers.runtime.ts --- a/extensions/telegram/src/bot-handlers.runtime.ts +++ b/extensions/telegram/src/bot-handlers.runtime.ts @@ -111,6 +111,7 @@ export const registerTelegramHandlers = ({ @@ -1501,6 +1502,79 @@ export const registerTelegramHandlers = ({ diff --git a/extensions/telegram/src/bot-native-commands.ts b/extensions/telegram/src/bot-native-commands.ts --- a/extensions/telegram/src/bot-native-commands.ts +++ b/extensions/telegram/src/bot-native-commands.ts @@ -9,6 +9,7 @@ import { resolveMarkdownTableMode } from "openclaw/plugin-sdk/config-runtime"; docs/experiments/plans/acp-node-slice3-fixup7-summary.md, docs/ex [changed files truncated after 2000 chars] Body: ```markdown ## Summary - Problem: OpenClaw did not yet have a complete **node-backed ACP runtime** where the gateway owns the durable state and a paired node acts only as the execution worker. - Why it matters: without that split, restart/replay/reconnect behavior is too dependent on the live process, which makes lease fencing, terminal resolution, and outward delivery recovery harder to reason about and prove. - What this PR adds: - a durable ACP gateway store for sessions, runs, accepted worker events, leases, terminal state, replay targets, and projector checkpoints - gateway ingestion of ACP worker events coming back from paired nodes - a real node-host ACP worker bridge and gateway/node command plumbing - a durable ACP reply projection/replay path so restart recovery can resume from gateway state instead of only from the live iterator - startup recovery for incomplete ACP node runs - restart/reconnect/cancel/close race hardening with focused restart/replay test coverage - architecture / protocol / verification docs for the new backend - Landing-only follow-up: after rebasing onto current `main`, this PR also includes a few **test-only** isolation fixes in Feishu / Google / Google Chat extension tests that were needed to make the landing branch green. Those are not ACP runtime behavior changes. ## What changed by area ### 1) Durable ACP gateway state New ACP store/state plumbing under `src/acp/store/**`: - persists ACP sessions and runs - persists accepted worker events - tracks leases / lease epochs - resolves canonical terminal state on the gateway - stores replay targets and projector checkpoints separately from the runtime cursor This is the foundation that lets the gateway stay authoritative even when execution happens on a paired node. ### 2) Gateway ↔ node ACP worker path Main implementation areas: - `src/gateway/**` - `src/infra/node-commands.ts` - `src/node-host/**` This adds the node-backed ACP execution path: - gateway can start/control ACP work on a paired node - node-host can run the ACP worker role - worker events stream back to the gateway over the existing node transport - gateway remains the source of truth for run state and terminal resolution ### 3) Durable reply projection and replay Main implementation areas: - `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.ts` - `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp-delivery.ts` - `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp-replay.ts` This PR moves ACP outward delivery onto a durable replayable path: - [body truncated after 2500 chars] ``` Comments/context: ```markdown - jorgensandhaug at 2026-03-17T10:54:42Z: /landpr - greptile-apps at 2026-03-17T10:59:50Z:

Greptile Summary

This PR lands the ACP node runtime (slices 1–4), wiring the gateway-side durable store, lease lifecycle, turn delivery, and crash-recovery replay paths to a real node-host worker bridge. The architecture is well thought-out: the gateway store persists every event and checkpoint to disk, the `AcpDurableProjectionRunner` replays from that store on restart, and a layered prepared/pending/settled synthetic-final checkpoint scheme provides at-least-once TTS delivery with transcript-backed idempotency. Key observations from the review: - **Dead code in `src/gateway/server-node-events.ts`** (line 268): The `if (handled) { return; }` guard immediately follows a `if (!handled) { throw ... }` block, making it unreachable. Should simply be `return;`. - **`isRecord` inconsistency in `src/acp/store/gateway-events.ts`** (line 50): The helper does not exclude arrays (`!Array.isArray(value)`), unlike the identical helper in `src/acp/runtime/acp-node.ts`. Downstream validators still reject the payload, but with a misleading field-level error rather than "must decode to an object". - The `recordSettledSyntheticFinalCheckpoint` fallback chain in `dispatch-acp-replay.ts` is intentionally complex and is correctly designed for crash recovery. - Error-swallowing in `dispatch-acp.ts` (swallowing `turnError` when the durable run has a "failed" terminal) is intentional and correct [comments/context truncated after 1500 chars] ```

Body:
```markdown
## Summary

Keep the TUI visibly busy while a run is waiting on a follow-up event after `sessions_yield`.

Before this change, the TUI could appear idle even though work was still effectively in flight. The gap showed up when the final assistant message contained the `sessions_yield` follow-up context marker: the run had ended intentionally, but the UI status dropped back to an idle-looking state.

## What changed

- add `awaiting follow-up` as a busy TUI activity state
- detect the `sessions_yield` follow-up marker in final assistant output
- preserve `awaiting follow-up` when reloading session history
- clear stale `awaiting follow-up` state back to `idle` when history no longer contains the marker
- add focused tests for both live event handling and history restore behavior

## Files

- `src/tui/tui.ts`
- `src/tui/tui-event-handlers.ts`
- `src/tui/tui-session-actions.ts`
- `src/tui/tui-event-handlers.test.ts`
- `src/tui/tui-session-actions.test.ts`

## Validation

- direct smoke harness passed for:
  - final event -> `awaiting follow-up`
  - history restore -> `awaiting follow-up`
  - stale follow-up state -> `idle`
- local lint/format checks passed on the touched files

## Notes

A focused local Vitest run hit a Node internal assertion in this environment, so the behavior was additionally checked with a direct harness against the patched TUI logic.
```

- Maintainers can comment `@clawsweeper explain` to ask for more context, or `@clawsweeper stop` to stop active automation.

</details>

The PR adds an `awaiting follow-up` TUI activity state, detects a `sessions_yield` context marker in final assistant/history text, renders that as a busy status, and adds focused TUI tests.

Today they must parse JSONL transcripts after the fact. This change surfaces the data directly in the `agent.wait` WebSocket response, which is the natural integration point for synchronous orchestration flows. Paperclip's adapter (`@paperclipai/adapter-openclaw-gateway`) already has `parseUsage()` ready to consume `usage`, `costUsd`, `provider`, and `model` from response metadata — it just gets `undefined` today. See Paperclip PR [#884](https://github.com/paperclipai/paperclip/pull/884). ## Design The `meta` field is fully optional and backward-compatible — it is omitted when no usage data is available (e.g., error runs that never reached the LLM). ```typescript meta?: { usage?: { input: number; output: number; cacheRead?: number; cacheWrite?: number }; lastCallUsage?: { input: number; output: number }; costUsd?: number; provider?: string; model?: string; }; ``` ## Files changed - `src/gateway/server-methods/agent-wait-dedupe.ts` — New `AgentWaitUsageMeta` type, extended snapshot type, `extractUsageMeta()` parser - `src/gateway/server-methods/agent.ts` — `buildUsageMetaFromRunResult()` helper, meta injection in dispatch + both wait response paths ## Security This change does not alter any trust boundary. The `meta` field is only included in `agent.wait` responses to authenticated gateway callers (trusted operators per the security policy). No secrets, credentials, or sensitive data are exposed — only token counts and estimated cost. ## Test plan - [ ] Existing `agent-wait-dedupe.test.ts` tests pass (backward-compatible) - [ ] `meta` appears in `agent.wait` response when a run completes with usage data - [ ] `meta` is omitted when no agent meta is available (error paths) - [ ] `costUsd` is populated when model cost config exists, omitted otherwise 🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com [body truncated after 2500 chars] ``` Comments/context: ```markdown - greptile-apps at 2026-03-18T05:45:25Z:

Body:
```markdown
## Summary
Adds distributed ACP session locking with Redis support and fail-closed behavior when Redis is configured but unavailable.

## What changed
- added `SessionLockManager` abstraction
- added `LocalSessionLockManager`
- added `RedisSessionLockManager`
- added `FailClosedSessionLockManager`
- wired lock acquire/release/renew into `tryDispatchAcpReply`
- skip ACP dispatch with `acp_execution_locked` when lock is contended or acquire fails
- documented:
  - `OPENCLAW_ACP_SESSION_LOCK_REDIS_URL`
  - `OPENCLAW_ACP_SESSION_LOCK_TTL_MS`
- added unit + dispatch integration tests

## Locking behavior
- no Redis configured → local in-process lock manager
- Redis configured and healthy → Redis lock manager
- Redis configured but init fails → fail closed, no fallback to local
- release only if owner matches
- renew only if owner matches
- renew is best-effort during long-running turns

## Validation
- `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile`
- `pnpm tsgo`
- `pnpm vitest run src/auto-reply/reply/session-lock-manager.test.ts`
- `pnpm vitest run src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.test.ts`
```

  - `LocalSessionLockManager`
  - `RedisSessionLockManager`
  - `FailClosedSessionLockManager`
- wires lock acquire / renew / release into `tryDispatchAcpReply`
- skips ACP dispatch with `acp_execution_locked` when lock is contended or acquire fails
- documents:
  - `OPENCLAW_ACP_SESSION_LOCK_REDIS_URL`
  - `OPENCLAW_ACP_SESSION_LOCK_TTL_MS`
- adds unit + dispatch integration coverage

**Behavior:**
- no Redis configured → local in-process lock manager
- Redis configured and healthy → Redis lock manager
- Redis configured but init fails → fail closed, no fallback to local
- Redis release/renew are owner-checked
- renew is best-effort for long-running turns and logs renew failure/error

ds4_description: Adds distributed ACP session locking with Redis support and fail-closed behavior to prevent concurrent ACP execution across workers. Introduces SessionLockManager abstraction (local/Redis/fail-closed variants) wired into the ACP dispatch flow, with owner-checked acquire/release/renew semantics. The lock is enforced at the ACP dispatch boundary to skip concurrent runs with acp_execution_locked.

target: openclaw/openclaw github_issue #53319: [Bug]: ACP concurrent session spawns — first agent fails to launch CC process
github_context:
GitHub item:
- Repository: openclaw/openclaw
- Type: issue
- Number: 53319
- URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/53319
- Title: [Bug]: ACP concurrent session spawns — first agent fails to launch CC process
- State: OPEN
- Author: bluk1020
- Labels: P2, clawsweeper:no-new-fix-pr, clawsweeper:needs-maintainer-review, clawsweeper:needs-info, impact:session-state, impact:message-loss, issue-rating: 🦐 gold shrimp

Body:
```markdown
## Summary

When spawning two ACP sessions (`sessions_spawn runtime:"acp"`) in rapid succession (~37s apart), the first agent's CC process never starts while the second eventually works fine.

## Environment

- OpenClaw `2026.3.22`
- acpx `0.1.16`
- ACP backend: `acpx`, defaultAgent: `claude`
- Node 22.22.1, macOS (arm64)

## Steps to Reproduce

1. From a main session, call `sessions_spawn` twice in quick succession:
   - Agent 1 spawned at `02:22:28 UTC`
   - Agent 2 spawned at `02:23:05 UTC` (37s later)
2. Both return `status: "accepted"` with valid `childSessionKey` and `streamLogPath`

## Observed Behavior

**Agent 1** (`0a73e5e1`):
- `lifecycle:start` emitted at 02:22:28
- `stall` warning at 02:23:28 (60s, no output)
- **No `assistant_delta` events ever appear** — CC process never produced any output
- `ps aux | grep claude.*reachfar` shows no matching process

**Agent 2** (`ebf86699`):
- `lifecycle:start` emitted at 02:23:05
- `stall` warning at 02:24:05 (60s cold start)
- `assistant_delta` appears at 02:25:17 — CC **starts working normally**
- Progress updates continue ("Now let me read the specific files...")

## Expected Behavior

Both agents should launch successfully. A ~37s gap between spawns should not cause the first to silently fail.

## Stream Log Evidence

**Agent 1 stream log** (3 lines total, never progresses):
```
lifecycle:start → system_event:stall → (nothing)
```

**Agent 2 stream log** (resumes after cold start):
```
lifecycle:start → system_event:stall → assistant_delta ("Now let me read...") → system_event:resumed → ...
```

## Workaround

Falling back to direct `claude --print --permission-mode bypassPermissions` via exec works reliably for parallel spawns (tested 3 concurrent agents successfully).

## Analysis

The acpx backend appears to have a race condition during concurrent session initialization. The first session's CC process is either:
1. Never spawned (acpx loses the session init in concurrent handling)
2. Spawned but immediately crashes without emitting any output
3. Blocked waiting on a resource the second session acquires first

Since the session is "accepted" and `lifecycle:start` fires, the issue is downstream of session registration — likely in the acpx CLI launch path.

## Related

- #52878 — ACP backend registration regression
- #53256 — ACP completion relay fix (different bug, addresses result routing)
- #49782 — RFC: ACP completion relay
```

Body:
```markdown
### Bug type

Regression / runtime bug

### Summary

`sessions_spawn(runtime="subagent")` appears to ignore documented subagent thinking inheritance/defaults and initializes spawned sessions with `thinkingLevel: low`.

On this install:
- parent session thinking is `high`
- per-agent `subagents.thinking` is `high`
- target agent `thinkingDefault` may also be `high`
- but spawned `runtime="subagent"` sessions still begin with `thinking_level_change ... low`

An explicit `sessions_spawn(..., thinking: "high")` **does** work, so the failure appears to be in the inherited/default resolution path, not in the thinking system generally.

### Steps to reproduce

Config excerpt:

```json
{
  "agents": {
    "list": [
      {
        "id": "architect",
        "thinkingDefault": "high",
        "subagents": {
          "thinking": "high",
          "allowAgents": ["reviewer", "scribe"]
        }
      },
      {
        "id": "reviewer",
        "model": "github-copilot/claude-sonnet-4.6",
        "thinkingDefault": "high"
      }
    ]
  }
}
```

From a parent agent session whose transcript shows:

```json
{"type":"thinking_level_change", ..., "thinkingLevel":"high"}
```

spawn a child without explicit thinking:

```text
sessions_spawn({
  agentId: "reviewer",
  runtime: "subagent",
  mode: "run",
  task: "Reply with exactly: TEST_OK"
})
```

Then inspect the child session JSONL.

### Expected behavior

The spawned subagent should resolve thinking according to the documented/defaulted chain:
- explicit `sessions_spawn.thinking` if provided
- per-agent `agents.list[].subagents.thinking`
- global `agents.defaults.subagents.thinking`
- otherwise inherit the caller
- then fallback only if none of the above applies

In the repro above, the child should start at `high`, not `low`.

### Actual behavior

The child session starts with:

```json
{"type":"thinking_level_change", ..., "thinkingLevel":"low"}
```

Observed evidence from this install:

Parent architect session:

```json
{"type":"session","version":3,"id":"c58a505a-b235-492a-8959-1af6a03f692a","timestamp":"2026-03-25T15:16:59.318Z","cwd":"/home/vrurg"}
{"type":"model_change","id":"01a4ace6","parentId":null,"timestamp":"2026-03-25T15:16:59.323Z","provider":"github-copilot","modelId":"gpt-5.4"}
{"type":"thinking_level_change","id":"80963d1f","parentId":"01a4ace6","timestamp":"2026-03-25T15:16:59.323Z","thinkingLevel":"high"}
```

Reviewer child spawned without explicit `thinking`:

```json
{"type":"session","version"

[body truncated after 2500 chars]
```

`resolveSubagentThinkingOverride` in `dist/subagent-spawn-CyV5i9yl.js:171-191` only consults two fields:

function resolveSubagentThinkingOverride(params) {

  // ...
}
```

It reads:
1. `agents.list[target].subagents.thinking`
2. `agents.defaults.subagents.thinking`

It does **not** read the target agent's top-level `thinkingDefault`, nor `agents.defaults.thinkingDefault`. Every other thinking-level resolution path in the codebase calls the shared helper `resolveThinkingDefault` (in `dist/model-selection-CTdyYoio.js:11+`) which does honor `thinkingDefault`.

## Hit during

A 108-run benchmark where `sessions_spawn` was intended to orchestrate Pi/Codex runners with `thinkingDefault: "xhigh"` on each target agent. Workaround was to drop `sessions_spawn` entirely and use a CLI subprocess driver (`openclaw agent --json`), because duplicating `subagents.thinking` on every agent plus passing `thinkingLevel` explicitly on every call became fragile.

Call `resolveThinkingDefault` (or inli

GitHub item: - Repository: openclaw/openclaw - Type: pull_request - Number: 56532 - URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/56532 - Title: memory-lancedb: add configurable timeout/retry for embedding calls - State: OPEN - Author: amittell - Labels: docs, extensions: memory-lancedb, size: M, proof: supplied, proof: sufficient, P2, rating: 🦐 gold shrimp, merge-risk: 🚨 availability, status: ⏳ waiting on author - Changed file count available to wrapper: 6 - Changed files: CHANGELOG.md, docs/plugins/memory-lancedb.md, extensions/memory-lancedb/config.test.ts, extensions/memory-lancedb/config.ts, extensions/memory-lancedb/index.ts, extensions/memory-lancedb/openclaw.plugin.json Body: ```markdown Replaces #56517 (closed, could not reopen). - Add embedding.timeoutMs (default 10s) and embedding.maxRetries (default 1) to memory-lancedb plugin config - Prevents cascading Connection error failures during API rate-limit storms - Also bumps LaunchAgent ThrottleInterval from 1s to 10s ## Real behavior proof - **Behavior or issue addressed**: `memory-lancedb`'s OpenAI-SDK embedding client had no operator-tunable timeout/retry budget. A hung or 5xx-storming embedding backend blocks on the SDK default (600000ms) and stalls every agent turn that triggers auto-recall. This PR lets operators bound it via `embedding.timeoutMs` / `embedding.maxRetries`. Per ClawSweeper review, the bounds now apply ONLY when explicitly configured; unset installs keep the SDK defaults (no upgrade behavior change). - **Real environment tested**: local `openclaw` checkout (upgrade onto current upstream/main) with the bundled `openai` SDK the plugin loads at runtime, exercised against a blackhole TCP endpoint that accepts the connection but never responds (simulating a hung embedding backend). - **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: ```bash # blackhole server + the same OpenAI client the plugin constructs, with the # configured bound applied: node ./embed-timeout-proof.mjs # full script in PR discussion # core: new OpenAI({ baseURL: , timeout: 2000, maxRetries: 1 }) # .embeddings.create({ model, input }) ``` - **Evidence after fix**: captured live from the blackhole run via `node` against the bundled `openai` SDK in `~/.openclaw`/local checkout: ``` blackhole embedding endpoint listening at http://127.0.0.1:60765/v1 (never responds) configured timeoutMs=2000 maxRetries=1: failed after 4520ms — APIConnectionTimeoutError: Request timed out. ``` The configured bound aborts at 2000ms, retries once, and surfaces `APIConnectionTimeoutError` after ~4.5s total — instead of hanging on the SDK default 600000ms. The deployed wiring in `extensions/memory-lancedb/index.ts` now spreads `timeout`/`maxRetries` into the `new OpenAI({...})` constructor only when set (`...(timeoutMs !== undefined ? { timeout: timeoutMs } : {})`), so unconfigured installs retain SDK defaults. - **Observed result after fix**: With `embedding.timeoutMs` set, a hung endpoint fails fast at the configured bound. With it unset, the OpenAI SDK default (600000ms / 2 retries) is preserved unchanged — no regression for installs with slow-but-working endpoints (the upgrade r [body truncated after 2500 chars] ``` Comments/context: ```markdown - greptile-apps at 2026-03-28T17:18:08Z:

Greptile Summary

This PR adds configurable `embedding.timeoutMs` (default 10 s) and `embedding.maxRetries` (default 1) to the `memory-lancedb` plugin, propagating both into the OpenAI client constructor and as a per-request override. It also bumps the launchd `ThrottleInterval` from 1 s to 10 s to mitigate rapid crash-loop respawns. **Issues found:** - **Stale test assertion (`index.test.ts` lines 288–292):** The original `toHaveBeenCalledWith(params)` (no second arg) was not removed when the new assertion `toHaveBeenCalledWith(params, { timeout: 10_000 })` was added. Because `embeddingsCreate` is now always called with two arguments, the old assertion will fail — Vitest's `toHaveBeenCalledWith` requires an exact argument match. The old assertion needs to be deleted. **Other notes:** - Passing `timeout` both to the OpenAI client constructor and as a per-request option is harmless (per-request wins); the constructor-level timeout acts as a safety net for any other incidental requests made through the same client instance. - Reducing `maxRetries` from the SDK default (2) to 1 is intentional and well-motivated for rate-limit storm mitigation. - The `ThrottleInterval` increase trades a slightly slower intentional restart for meaningful protection against rapid crash loops — reasonable for a daemon.

Confidence Score: 4/5

target: openclaw/openclaw github_issue #56613: [Feature]: Talk/Voice tab — agent/session switching + per-agent TTS voice
github_context:
GitHub item:
- Repository: openclaw/openclaw
- Type: issue
- Number: 56613
- URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/56613
- Title: [Feature]: Talk/Voice tab — agent/session switching + per-agent TTS voice
- State: OPEN
- Author: kenchen3000
- Labels: P3, clawsweeper:no-new-fix-pr, clawsweeper:fix-shape-clear, clawsweeper:needs-maintainer-review, clawsweeper:needs-product-decision, clawsweeper:source-repro, impact:session-state, issue-rating: 🦞 diamond lobster

Body:
```markdown
### Summary

Two related feature requests for the Android (and iOS/macOS) Talk/Voice mode:

## 1. Voice/Talk tab agent switching

Currently the Voice tab is hardcoded to the `main` session (default agent). The Chat tab already has a session picker that lets users switch between agents, but the Voice tab does not.

**Request:** Allow users to select which agent to talk to in the Voice tab, similar to how the Chat tab session picker works.

**Use case:** Users with multiple agents (e.g., IT Director, Data Scientist, Marketing Director) want to have voice conversations with each of them, not just the default agent.

**Current behavior:** Voice tab always sends to `main` session via `chat.send`. Switching sessions in the Chat tab does not affect the Voice tab.

**Expected behavior:** Voice tab respects the selected session/agent, or has its own agent picker.

**Code reference:** `VoiceTabScreen.kt` has no session selection. `TalkModeManager.kt` uses `mainSessionKey` which defaults to `"main"`. The Chat tab's session switcher (`ChatSheetContent.kt`) already demonstrates the pattern.

## 2. Per-agent TTS voice configuration

Currently TTS voice is a global setting (`messages.tts.microsoft.voice` or `talk.voiceId`). All agents share the same voice.

**Request:** Allow per-agent voice configuration so each agent can have a distinct voice identity.

**Use case:** A user running multiple AI agents wants each agent to sound different — e.g., a deep male voice for one, a warm female voice for another. This makes multi-agent setups more intuitive and immersive.

**Proposed config example:**
```json
{
  "agents": {
    "list": [
      {
        "id": "main",
        "talk": { "voiceId": "voice-id-1" }
      },
      {
        "id": "it-director",
        "talk": { "voiceId": "voice-id-2" }
      }
    ]
  }
}
```

Or via workspace-level config in each agent's workspace files.

### Impact

These two features together would enable natural voice conversations with multiple AI agents, each with their own voice identity — a significant step toward multi-agent voice interaction.

### Environment

- OpenClaw Android app (built from source, v2026.3.20)
- Gateway on macOS (Mac Mini)
- Multiple agents configured (5+ agents)
```

target: openclaw/openclaw github_issue #58411: sessions_spawn lacks --bind here equivalent — agent cannot bind ACP session to existing Discord thread
github_context:
GitHub item:
- Repository: openclaw/openclaw
- Type: issue
- Number: 58411
- URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/58411
- Title: sessions_spawn lacks --bind here equivalent — agent cannot bind ACP session to existing Discord thread
- State: OPEN
- Author: brew-agent
- Labels: P2, clawsweeper:fix-shape-clear, clawsweeper:queueable-fix, clawsweeper:source-repro, impact:session-state, impact:message-loss, issue-rating: 🦞 diamond lobster

Body:
```markdown
## Summary

When a user is in an existing Discord thread and asks the agent to start a coding session, the correct UX is `/acp spawn <agent> --bind here` — which attaches the ACP session to the current thread in place.

However, this slash command can only be run by a human. If the user asks the **agent** to spawn the session, the agent has no programmatic equivalent. `sessions_spawn` only supports `thread: true`, which always **creates a new thread** — which is the wrong behavior here.

## Use case

1. User and agent are having a discussion in an existing Discord thread
2. User says: _"Start a Codex session on this"_
3. Expected: agent binds an ACP session to the current thread — conversation continues in place
4. Actual: agent has to ask the user to manually run `/acp spawn <agent> --bind here`, breaking the flow

## Proposed solution

Add a `bindTo: "current"` option to `sessions_spawn` that maps to `--bind here` semantics — attaches the ACP session to the current thread without creating a new one:

```json
{
  "runtime": "acp",
  "mode": "session",
  "bindTo": "current",
  "task": "..."
}
```

## Related

- #41004 — same slash command vs `sessions_spawn` asymmetry on Telegram
```

target: openclaw/openclaw github_issue #59878: Session lane stuck in 'running' after run dies — sessions.abort + gateway restart fail to clear stale state
github_context:
GitHub item:
- Repository: openclaw/openclaw
- Type: issue
- Number: 59878
- URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/59878
- Title: Session lane stuck in 'running' after run dies — sessions.abort + gateway restart fail to clear stale state
- State: OPEN
- Author: Cuttingwater
- Labels: P1, clawsweeper:no-new-fix-pr, clawsweeper:source-repro, clawsweeper:linked-pr-open, impact:session-state, impact:message-loss, issue-rating: 🦞 diamond lobster

Body:
```markdown
## Bug Description

Session lanes get permanently stuck in `status: "running"` after the actual LLM run dies. The stale running state is not cleared by:

1. `sessions.abort` RPC (returns `{"status": "no-active-run"}` confirming the run is dead)
2. Gateway restart (stale state persists through restart)

This blocks the session lane indefinitely — every new message queues behind the dead lock and is never processed.

## Scope

This is broader than #16331 (compaction-triggered lane jams). Any run that dies without proper lifecycle cleanup can cause this — we observed it across webchat, cron, home-node, and default sessions simultaneously.

## Evidence (2026-04-02, version 2026.3.24)

**11 zombie sessions found in a single check**, some stuck for over a week:

```
agent:main:home-tablet-kitchen:  running for 10,571 min (7.3 days)
agent:main:home-tablet-test2:    running for 9,983 min
agent:main:home-tablet-test3:    running for 9,980 min
agent:main:default:              running for 2,888 min (2 days)
agent:main:web_..._7ad797481643: running for 1,309 min
agent:main:cron:721fa3b5-...:    running for 949 min
agent:home:default:              running for 203 min
agent:home:debug:                running for 189 min
```

Every single one returned `"no-active-run"` on `sessions.abort`.

**Gateway logs show lane wait warnings going back hours:**

```
lane wait exceeded: lane=session:agent:main:main waitedMs=1309962 queueAhead=2
lane wait exceeded: lane=session:agent:main:main waitedMs=842773 queueAhead=1
lane wait exceeded: lane=session:agent:main:main waitedMs=539336 queueAhead=0
```

## Impact

- Multiple users unable to get responses on webchat (messages silently queue forever)
- No error surfaced to the user — just permanent silence
- Affects any session type (webchat, cron, home-node, default)

## Workaround

Sending `/new` via `sessions.send` RPC clears the stale state:

```bash
openclaw gateway call sessions.send \
  --params '{"key":"agent:main:main","message":"/new"}' \
  --timeout 15000
```

We have deployed a watchdog script that runs every 10 minutes, detects sessions stuck in "running" beyond 20 minutes with no active run, and auto-recovers via `/new`.

## Expected Behavior

1. `sessions.abort` should clear stale running state when no active run exists
2. Gateway restart should reconcile session status against actual run state
3. Ideally: a session-lane timeout that auto-recovers after a configurable threshold

github_context:

- State: OPEN
- Author: Lukavyi
- Labels: P2, clawsweeper:no-new-fix-pr, clawsweeper:fix-shape-clear, clawsweeper:needs-maintainer-review, clawsweeper:needs-product-decision, clawsweeper:needs-security-review, impact:session-state, impact:security, issue-rating: 🌊 off-meta tidepool

Body:
```markdown
[Feature]: Per-DM/per-chat default ACP binding for auto-spawning ACP sessions on new topics

## Problem

When using Telegram DM forum topics with ACP agents (Claude Code, Codex, etc.), every new topic requires manually running `/acp spawn claude` or asking the main agent to spawn an ACP session. There is no way to configure a DM chat so that new topics automatically bind to an ACP agent.

## Proposed solution

Add a `defaultAcp` config option to `TelegramDirectConfig` (and potentially `TelegramGroupConfig`) that auto-spawns an ACP session when a new topic/thread is created.

### Config example

```json5
{
  "channels": {
    "telegram": {
      "direct": {
        "63448508": {
          "defaultAcp": {
            "agent": "claude",
            "mode": "persistent",
            "cwd": "/workspace/default"
          }
        }
      }
    }
  }
}
```

### Behavior

1. User creates a new topic in Telegram DM with the bot
2. On first message in the new topic, OpenClaw detects no existing ACP binding
3. OpenClaw auto-spawns an ACP session using `defaultAcp` config
4. The topic is bound to that ACP session
5. All subsequent messages in the topic route directly to the ACP agent
6. Topics with explicit per-topic config (`topics.<id>.agentId`) should override `defaultAcp`

### Scope

- `TelegramDirectConfig.defaultAcp` - for DM topics
- `TelegramGroupConfig.defaultAcp` - for group forum topics
- Could also be a top-level `TelegramAccountConfig.defaultAcp` for account-wide default

### Alternatives considered

- **Static bindings per topic:** Works but requires manual config for each topic ID upfront
- **Main agent with auto-spawn system prompt:** Wastes a main agent turn just to spawn ACP
- **`/acp spawn` in every new topic:** Manual friction, defeats the purpose of topics as lightweight sessions

### Related

- Thread bindings: `threadBindings.spawnAcpSessions`
- Existing ACP binding model: `bindings[].type="acp"`
- Per-topic agentId routing: `topics.<id>.agentId`

```

github_context:
GitHub item:
- Repository: openclaw/openclaw
- Type: issue
- Number: 60979
- URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/60979
- Title: feature: sessions_spawn ACP delivery to channel (stream output to Zulip/Discord topic)
- State: OPEN
- Author: app/spark-of-imagination
- Labels: P2, clawsweeper:no-new-fix-pr, clawsweeper:fix-shape-clear, clawsweeper:needs-maintainer-review, clawsweeper:needs-product-decision, clawsweeper:source-repro, impact:message-loss, issue-rating: 🦞 diamond lobster

Body:
```markdown
## Summary

When spawning an ACP session via `sessions_spawn` (e.g. OpenCode/Codex), there is no way to direct the session's output to a specific channel conversation or topic. This makes it impossible to observe long-running agent tasks in real-time from a chat surface without the parent agent acting as a relay.

## Desired Workflow

```
User ↔ Mimiron (main agent) — discuss task
Mimiron → spawns Jeeves
Jeeves → spawns OpenCode ACP session, output bound to Zulip stream topic
User watches progress live in Zulip topic
Jeeves/Mimiron free to do other things
```

## Current State

- `streamTo: "parent"` exists but only relays events back to the spawning agent (not to a channel)
- `cron` jobs support `delivery.mode: "announce"` which routes output to a channel — no equivalent exists for `sessions_spawn` ACP
- The Zulip channel plugin already implements `ChannelConversationBindingSupport` with `supportsCurrentConversationBinding: true` and a full `SessionBindingAdapter` — the adapter infrastructure is ready

## Proposed Solution

Add a `delivery` option to `sessions_spawn` for `runtime="acp"` sessions, analogous to cron job delivery:

```json
{
  "runtime": "acp",
  "agentId": "opencode",
  "task": "...",
  "delivery": {
    "mode": "announce",
    "channel": "zulip",
    "to": "stream:Projects:agent-output"
  }
}
```

This would wire the ACP session output to the specified channel conversation, leveraging the existing `SessionBindingAdapter` infrastructure already present in channel plugins.

## References

- Internal research issue: whizzlelabs/sparkofimagination#23 (options A/B/C analysis)
- Zulip plugin binding adapter: `whizzlelabs/openclaw-zulip` — `src/bindings.ts` — `createZulipSessionBindingAdapter`, `zulipConversationBindingsSupport`
- Cron delivery precedent: `cron` jobs support `delivery.mode: "announce"` for channel output routing

```

target: openclaw/openclaw github_issue #62769: [Feature]: Support ACP configured bindings for Telegram DMs (not just groups/topics)
github_context:
GitHub item:
- Repository: openclaw/openclaw
- Type: issue
- Number: 62769
- URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/62769
- Title: [Feature]: Support ACP configured bindings for Telegram DMs (not just groups/topics)
- State: OPEN
- Author: richardmqq
- Labels: P2, clawsweeper:source-repro, impact:session-state, impact:message-loss, issue-rating: 🦞 diamond lobster

Body:
```markdown
## Summary

Telegram ACP configured bindings (`bindings[]` with `type: "acp"`) only work for group chats and forum topics, not for direct messages (DMs). This prevents using persistent ACP sessions in Telegram DM conversations with dedicated agent bots.

## Root Cause

In `normalizeTelegramAcpConversationId()`, the function explicitly rejects any `chatId` that does not start with `"-"` (i.e., group chat IDs):

```javascript
function normalizeTelegramAcpConversationId(conversationId) {
    const parsed = parseTelegramTopicConversation({ conversationId });
    if (!parsed || !parsed.chatId.startsWith("-")) return null;
    // ...
}
```

This means DM peer IDs (positive integers like `6689123501`) are filtered out, and the binding is silently ignored.

Similarly, `matchTelegramAcpConversation()` checks `incoming.chatId.startsWith("-")`, which also rejects DM conversations.

## Expected Behavior

A configured binding like this should work for Telegram DMs:

```json
{
  "type": "acp",
  "agentId": "claude",
  "match": {
    "channel": "telegram",
    "accountId": "claude",
    "peer": { "kind": "direct", "id": "6689123501" }
  },
  "acp": { "mode": "persistent" }
}
```

When a user sends a message to the dedicated agent bot in DM, it should route through the ACP binding and create/resume an ACP harness session, just like it does for group topics.

## Actual Behavior

- The binding passes `openclaw config validate` ✅
- But at runtime, `normalizeTelegramAcpConversationId()` returns `null` for DM peer IDs
- The binding is silently ignored
- Messages fall through to native embedded runtime instead of ACP harness

## Use Case

We run dedicated Telegram bots for different ACP agents (Claude Code, Codex, etc.). Each bot has its own `accountId`. Users DM these bots expecting to interact with the actual ACP harness (e.g., Claude Code CLI). Without DM binding support, there is no way to persistently bind a Telegram DM conversation to an ACP session through config.

Currently, the only workaround is `/acp spawn` in a group topic, which defeats the purpose of having dedicated DM bots.

## Additional Context

- Gateway restart can cause existing ACP session metadata to be lost from `sessions.json`, making the DM → ACP routing even less reliable
- `agents.list[].runtime.type: "acp"` does NOT automatically route DM messages through ACP — it only provides defaults for `/acp spawn`
- The `acpManager.resolveSession()` check in `agentCommandInternal()` returns `kind: "none"` for 

[body truncated after 2500 chars]
```

github_context:

- Changed file count available to wrapper: 7
- Changed files: src/gateway/server-methods/send.test.ts, src/gateway/server-methods/send.ts, src/hooks/message-hook-mappers.test.ts, src/hooks/message-hook-mappers.ts, src/infra/outbound/deliver.test.ts, src/infra/outbound/deliver.ts, src/plugins/types.ts

Body:
```markdown
## Summary

Make `gateway call send` sufficient for one truthful guarded outbound message path.

This patch:
- passes outbound session identity into `message_sending` hook context when available
- includes `agentId` and `sessionKey` additively and backwards-compatibly
- surfaces guarded delivery cancellation explicitly instead of collapsing it to `No delivery result`

## Why

Today `gateway call send` reaches the shared outbound delivery path and fires `message_sending`, but the hook context drops outbound identity. That makes guard plugins unable to make agent-scoped decisions, and the gateway then hides the real cancellation behind a generic `No delivery result`.

This patch keeps the route narrow:
- it does not unify direct CLI send paths
- it only fixes the gateway send path so one canonical guarded/audited outbound path is usable

## What changed

- add optional `agentId` / `sessionKey` to plugin message hook context
- pass outbound session identity from delivery into `message_sending`
- add an additive cancellation callback on outbound delivery
- have gateway send surface guarded cancellation explicitly

## Tests

- gateway send path reaches `message_sending` with agent identity
- guarded cancellation is surfaced explicitly
- successful delivery path still returns a delivery result

## Live verification

Verified on a real standard-local runtime with Telegram:
1. no credential -> blocked with explicit guarded denial
2. granted credential -> delivered with real provider result
3. revoked credential -> blocked again with explicit guarded denial

```

target: openclaw/openclaw github_issue #63229: Bug: Gateway falsely marks healthy local vLLM endpoints as timed out/overloaded, causing 1–23 min fallback cascades
github_context:
GitHub item:
- Repository: openclaw/openclaw
- Type: issue
- Number: 63229
- URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/63229
- Title: Bug: Gateway falsely marks healthy local vLLM endpoints as timed out/overloaded, causing 1–23 min fallback cascades
- State: OPEN
- Author: clawdia-lobster
- Labels: P1, clawsweeper:no-new-fix-pr, clawsweeper:needs-maintainer-review, clawsweeper:needs-info, impact:auth-provider, impact:crash-loop, issue-rating: 🦐 gold shrimp

Body:
```markdown
## Summary

The gateway's model-fallback/routing subsystem incorrectly marks healthy, responsive local vLLM endpoints as "timed out" or "overloaded", causing cascading fallback chains that take **1–23 minutes** to resolve. The endpoints themselves respond in **0.27–0.93s** when tested directly via curl.

## Environment

- OpenClaw: 2026.4.5 (container, Linux 6.12.63 x64)
- Gateway: loopback bind, port 18789
- Local vLLM endpoints:
  - `vllm-8001` (gemma4, 27B) on jupiter.wg.local:8001 — dedicated GPU
  - `vllm-7002` (qwen3.5-27b) on jupiter.wg.local:7002 — dedicated GPU
- Remote providers: Novita (GLM-5, Kimi), DeepInfra (Kimi), Anthropic (Sonnet)
- Config: `agents.defaults.timeoutSeconds: 1200`, `agents.defaults.llm.idleTimeoutSeconds: 300`

## Observed Behaviour

### 1. Endpoints are fast (direct curl, concurrent)

Both GPUs idle, tested concurrently:

| Endpoint | Avg latency (5 reqs) |
|----------|---------------------|
| vllm-8001/gemma4 | **0.28s** |
| vllm-7002/qwen3.5-27b | **0.91s** |

### 2. Gateway marks them as timed out or overloaded

From gateway logs, model fallback decisions for today:

**Failure reasons:**
- `timeout`: 17 occurrences
- `unknown`: 8
- `overloaded`: 2

**Error previews:**
- `LLM request timed out.`: 12
- `Gateway is draining for restart; new tasks are not accepted`: 8
- `cron: job execution timed out`: 4
- `Live session model switch requested: <model>`: 2
- `Request was aborted.`: 1

### 3. Fallback chains take minutes

Example fallback chains from today's logs:

| Run ID | Chain | Total time |
|--------|-------|-----------|
| `7c914aae` | qwen→timeout → Kimi→timeout → gemma→timeout → **Sonnet✓** | **23.4 min** |
| `21ca97c0` | gemma→timeout → **qwen✓** | **4.1 min** |
| `0cb06206` | gemma→timeout → **Kimi✓** | **56.6s** |
| `66f5e9e5` | gemma→timeout → **GLM-5✓** | **80.6s** |

### 4. Gateway can't even spawn subagents

Attempting `sessions_spawn` returns:
```
gateway timeout after 10000ms
Gateway target: ws://127.0.0.1:18789
```

Meanwhile, direct curl to the same endpoints returns in <1s.

### 5. "Overloaded" misclassification

The gateway logs show `reason: "overloaded"` with `errorPreview: "Live session model switch requested: novita/zai-org/glm-4.7"`. A **session model mismatch** is being classified as a provider overload — the gateway is conflating an internal session state error with provider unavailability.

## Expected Behaviour

- Requests to healthy, sub-second local endpoints should not time out
- Session model 

[body truncated after 2500 chars]
```

target: openclaw/openclaw github_pr #64181: fix(hooks): reject error responses from slug generator and strip post-truncation dashes
github_context:
GitHub item: - Repository: openclaw/openclaw - Type: pull_request - Number: 64181 - URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/64181 - Title: fix(hooks): reject error responses from slug generator and strip post-truncation dashes - State: OPEN - Author: Cypherm - Labels: size: S, triage: needs-real-behavior-proof, P2, rating: 🧂 unranked krab, merge-risk: 🚨 session-state, status: 📣 needs proof - Changed file count available to wrapper: 2 - Changed files: src/hooks/llm-slug-generator.test.ts, src/hooks/llm-slug-generator.ts Body: ```markdown ## Summary - **Problem**: When the embedded agent run inside `generateSlugViaLLM` fails (e.g. provider quota or auth error), the failure can come back as a payload text string instead of being thrown. The existing pipeline slugifies that error sentence and turns it into the memory filename. - **Why it matters**: I observed `memory/2026-04-10-missing-token-or-projectid-in-.md` get written to my `telegram-bot2` workspace when the configured Gemini CLI provider exhausted its daily Pro quota. The session memory file ended up indexed under a slug that's just the truncated error string, fragmenting the canonical daily file path that AGENTS.md tells agents to read. - **Secondary bug found while fixing it**: the slugify pipeline strips leading/trailing dashes BEFORE truncating to 30 characters. When truncation lands on a `-` boundary, the resulting slug ends in a dangling dash that nothing cleans up. This is what produces the trailing `-` in `2026-04-10-missing-token-or-projectid-in-.md`. - **What changed**: extracted a pure `slugifyLLMResponse` helper, added a sanity check (max words / max chars / no newlines) before slugifying, and moved the dash strip to AFTER `.slice(MAX_SLUG_LENGTH)`. - **What did NOT change (scope boundary)**: no changes to `generateSlugViaLLM`'s call signature, the embedded agent runner, or the session-memory handler. The fix is contained in `src/hooks/llm-slug-generator.ts` plus a new colocated test.
